project_overview.project_name: Nigeria Human Capital Opportunities for Prosperity and Equity - Governance (HOPE-GOV)
project_overview.project_development_objective: To support the Federal Republic of Nigeria in strengthening financial and human resource management in basic education and primary healthcare sectors
project_overview.beneficiaries: All 36 Nigerian states and Federal Capital Territory; basic education and primary healthcare sectors; teachers and healthcare workers; school children and patients; vulnerable populations including women and girls

geographic_scope.fragile_or_conflict_affected_areas: yes - Nigeria is flagged as a fragile state with conflict. The PAD explicitly marks the operation as involving both 'Fragile State(s)' and 'Conflict' in the financing modalities section. The country faces insecurity challenges, weak institutions, governance issues, and economic vulnerabilities that affect service delivery in education and health sectors

key_components.component_name: Results Area 1: Increased availability and effectiveness of financing for basic education and primary healthcare
key_components.description: Enhance states' access to UBE-IF resources; strengthen states' planning, budget preparation and execution; enhance budget making at local government level through adoption of national chart of accounts. Includes DLI 1 (Enhanced access of UBEC financing) and DLI 2 (Strengthened state budget planning and execution)

key_components.component_name: Results Area 2: Enhanced transparency and accountability for basic education and primary healthcare financing
key_components.description: Incentivize timely publication of citizen-friendly budgets and audit reports; improve transparency through publication of procurement processes; strengthen accountability at federal and state levels. Includes DLI 3 (Federal funding transparency) and DLI 4 (State funding transparency)

key_components.component_name: Results Area 3: Improved recruitment, deployment, and performance management of teachers and healthcare workers
key_components.description: Enhance sector and workforce planning; reduce staffing gaps and improve deployment practices; adopt systems to check payroll fraud using BVN and National Identity Numbers. Includes DLI 5 (Increased deployment of workers) and DLI 6 (Improved payroll management)

key_components.component_name: Investment Project Financing (IPF) Component - Technical Assistance
key_components.description: Support capacity building, program coordination, fiduciary management, and independent verification of results. Includes TA for federal and state governments on guidelines revision, baseline mapping, financial audits, citizen participation, climate-smart budgeting, and GBV prevention

implementation_arrangements.implementing_agency: Federal Ministry of Budget and Economic Planning (FMoBEP) as lead, with coordination from Federal Ministry of Education (FMoE), Federal Ministry of Health and Social Welfare (FMoHSW), Federal Ministry of Finance (FMoF), UBEC, and state-level counterparts across all 36 states
implementation_arrangements.coordination_mechanisms: National Steering Committee (inter-ministerial at federal level); Program Steering Committee (chaired by Minister of Budget and Economic Planning); National Program Coordination Unit (NPCU) within FMoBEP; State Technical Committees (STC) in each participating state; collaboration with Nigeria Governors Forum (NGF) for capacity building delivery
implementation_arrangements.procurement_approach: For PforR: Government's public procurement management structure based on Public Procurement Act 2007 and state legislation (UNCITRAL model-based). For IPF: World Bank Procurement Regulations for IPF Borrowers (September 2023). Procurement Plan prepared for first 18 months; e-procurement and Open Contracting Data Standards (OCDS) required for transparency
implementation_arrangements.monitoring_and_evaluation: Independent Verification Agent (IVA) to verify DLI achievement annually through Annual Performance Assessment (APA); NPCU responsible for M&E system development and implementation; quarterly, mid-year, and annual progress reports; M&E expert position in NPCU; building on existing M&E structures at sector ministries; publication of state performance data for peer learning

identified_risks.risk_description: High levels of state autonomy in Nigerian federalism; recent Supreme Court decision on local government autonomy may impact roles and commitment of state and local governments; potential resistance to change from stakeholders with vested interests
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: High-level government buy-in from new administrations; Program anchored on legislation (UBE Act 2004, National Health Act 2014) unlikely to be overturned; continued collaboration with Nigerian Governors Forum and Association of Local Governments of Nigeria (ALGON); socialization and stakeholder consultation; key political officer holders leading operations at federal level

identified_risks.risk_description: Uncertain growth outlook dependent on external factors and government policy response; fiscal deficit projected above 3% of GDP for 2024-2027; DLI 5 (hiring more workers) could be affected by fiscal contraction; dependence on oil prices and global economic conditions
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: World Bank monitoring of macro-economic outlook; support through RESET DPF (P501661) supporting macro-stability agenda; ARMOR PforR (P177308) strengthening federal revenue mobilization; coordinated approach to sustain medium-term fiscal outlook

identified_risks.risk_description: Multiple institutions involved with lack of coordination; weak M&E systems; limited capacity of implementing agencies; coordination challenges across federal-state-local levels; wide range of capacity across 36 states; risk of inadequate sequencing and coordination of HOPE interdependent programs
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Co-location of senior technical officers from sector ministries with NPCU; targeted TA under IPF component for capacity building; creation of Inter-Ministerial HOPE-IP Steering Committee with Ministers from FMoF, FMoBEP, FMoHSW, FMoE; capacity building support through trusted platforms (NGF); peer learning mechanisms

identified_risks.risk_description: Weak internal audit function focusing on pre-payment review; limited capacity of procurement staff; manual accounting records leading to errors; weak controls and compliance gaps; low budget execution rates due to non-cash-backed budgets and bureaucratic bottlenecks; risk of funds used for unintended purposes; non-application of World Bank debarment lists; delayed budget releases causing high deviations
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Risk-based internal audit approach with training; capacity building on Program fiduciary requirements; engagement of IVA with technical and audit skills; establishment of service standards for timely fund release (14 days); regular supervision and external audits; implementation of GRM; semi-annual fraud and corruption reporting; procurement performance audits at mid-term and end of program; verification of World Bank debarment lists before contract awards

identified_risks.risk_description: Need for coordination vertically (across levels of government) and horizontally (across sectors); technical design complexity; risk of inadequate sequencing of upstream reforms before downstream actions
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Program design anchored in Human Capital PEIR and government reform priorities; builds on government programs defining roles of different levels; states familiar with reforms from SFTAS; capacity building and TA through trusted platforms; peer learning and competition mechanisms

identified_risks.risk_description: Potential discrimination along ethnicity and religion in recruitment of teachers and health workers; risk of gender-based violence (GBV) and school-related GBV (SRGBV); potential healthcare waste generation from expanded PHC facilities; worker health and safety risks from rehabilitation works; potential influx of workers to communities; e-waste generation from office equipment procurement
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Transparent recruitment procedures to prevent ethnic/religious bias; gender balance requirements in recruitment and deployment; training on gender sensitivity and GBV prevention for newly recruited teachers and health workers; screening to prevent hiring of GBV perpetrators; establishment of grievance reporting and redress mechanisms; healthcare waste management strategy; environmental management strategy for schools; teacher training on environmental and social health and safety; Code of Conduct strengthening on GBV prevention; inclusive practices for students with disabilities

identified_risks.risk_description: Involvement of multiple stakeholders requiring high collaboration; resistance to change and status quo bias from government agencies, parastatals, teachers' unions, and government contractors; potential obstacles to implementation
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Coordination arrangement with FMoBEP as host ministry; TA by NGF Secretariat; collaboration through Governor's Forum; key political officer holders leading operations; consultation with key actors from identification to implementation; IPF component support for stakeholder consultation and communications

safeguards_and_social.environmental_category: Low (for IPF component); Moderate (for overall PforR)
triggered_safeguard_policies: ESS 1: Assessment and Management of Environmental and Social Risks and Impacts (Relevant)
ESS 10: Stakeholder Engagement and Information Disclosure (Relevant)
safeguards_and_social.grievance_redress_mechanism: yes - Multiple GRM mechanisms: (1) Program-specific GRM to be established by NPCU within 3 months of effectiveness; (2) World Bank's Grievance Redress Service (GRS) for complaints about World Bank non-compliance; (3) World Bank's Accountability Mechanism (Inspection Panel and Dispute Resolution Service); (4) Existing national level governance and anti-corruption arrangements; (5) State-level GRM with documentation of complaints and resolution; (6) Target of 80% of complaints addressed within 3 months; (7) Pathways for women to file complaints related to harassment, discrimination, and GBV
safeguards_and_social.gender_considerations: Program addresses gender equity in human resource management by sustaining balance in recruitment and deployment of male and female teachers and health workers. Currently 56% of basic education teachers are female and 65% of doctors and 87% of nurses in PHC are female. Program will target women for recruitment in PHC and basic education through incentive schemes and outreach programs. At least 30% of new recruits must be women. Program contributes to broader gender strategy of getting 200 million girls into school. Measures to prevent school-related gender-based violence (SRGBV) including teacher training on gender sensitivity, sexual harassment policies, and GBV recognition and response
vulnerable_groups: Children unable to read at age 10 (45 million of 60 million children aged 5-14)
Rural and hard-to-reach populations underserved by teachers and health workers
Communities affected by insecurity and conflict
Populations in fragile and conflict-affected areas

fcv_relevant_details.conflict_sensitivity: The program is designed with conflict sensitivity in mind. It addresses governance weaknesses and institutional capacity that contribute to fragility. By improving transparency, accountability, and service delivery in education and health, the program aims to build trust in state institutions and reduce grievances. The program includes mechanisms for grievance redress and stakeholder engagement. However, the PAD notes that insecurity is one of the factors affecting government revenues and economic growth. The program's focus on strengthening systems and institutions is intended to support state capacity and legitimacy, which are critical in fragile contexts. The program does not directly address security sector reform but supports the enabling environment for service delivery in insecure areas through improved financing and human resource management
fcv_relevant_details.fragility_context: Nigeria is explicitly identified as a fragile state with conflict. Key fragility factors mentioned include: (1) High dependence on oil with insufficient economic diversification; (2) Poor governance and weak institutions; (3) Low trust in state institutions; (4) Increasing insecurity affecting economic performance and government revenues; (5) Weak domestic oil production; (6) Low government revenues (6.7% of GDP in 2022, projected 8.6% in 2024); (7) High debt servicing (101.5% of revenues in 2022); (8) Perceptions of corruption; (9) Limited transparency and accountability; (10) Weak coordination between federal, state, and local governments; (11) Poor human capital outcomes (HCI score 0.36); (12) High under-5 mortality rate (114 per 1,000 live births); (13) 14.6 million out-of-school children; (14) Fiscal vulnerabilities and limited fiscal space; (15) Weak domestic institutions and systems
fcv_relevant_details.security_considerations: The PAD notes that insecurity is one of the recent shocks affecting the economy alongside COVID-19, oil price falls, and weak domestic oil production. Insecurity contributes to declining government revenues. The program does not directly address security issues but aims to strengthen institutional capacity and service delivery systems that can function in challenging environments. The program's focus on improving transparency, accountability, and resource management is intended to build institutional resilience. The program includes provisions for operating in difficult contexts through state-level implementation with flexibility and capacity building support. No specific security protocols are mentioned for implementation in conflict-affected areas, though the program's emphasis on grievance mechanisms and stakeholder engagement may help address grievances that could fuel conflict
fcv_relevant_details.displacement_or_refugees: no - The PAD does not mention displacement or refugee populations as a specific concern or target group. However, the program's focus on improving basic education and primary healthcare access could benefit displaced populations and refugees if they are present in participating states. The program's emphasis on equity and reaching underserved populations (rural and hard-to-reach areas) may indirectly support displaced or refugee communities. No specific provisions for displaced persons or refugees are mentioned in the program design
